sections.title: Grammatical Gender in Hindi
sections.explanation: Every Hindi noun has a grammatical gender: either masculine (पुल्लिंग) or feminine (स्त्रीलिंग). Unlike English, gender in Hindi applies to all nouns — not just living beings but also objects, concepts, and abstract ideas. The gender of a noun affects the form of adjectives, verbs, and postpositions used with it, so learning each noun's gender is essential for speaking correctly.
sections.tip: There is no neuter gender in Hindi — every noun is either masculine or feminine. When learning new vocabulary, always memorize the gender along with the word.

examples.en: water — masculine noun (despite ending in -ी, it is an exception)
table — feminine noun (no typical ending, must be memorized)

exercises.prompt: How many grammatical genders does Hindi have?
exercises.explanation: Hindi has exactly two grammatical genders: masculine (पुल्लिंग) and feminine (स्त्रीलिंग). Every noun belongs to one of these two categories, and there is no neuter gender.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a masculine noun?
exercises.explanation: लड़का (boy) is masculine. लड़की (girl) and मेज़ (table) are both feminine. Note that लड़का ends in -ा, which is a common masculine ending.

exercises.prompt: Why is knowing a noun's gender important in Hindi?
options: It changes the meaning of the noun
It only matters for polite speech
It affects adjectives, verbs, and postpositions
exercises.explanation: A noun's gender determines the form of associated adjectives, verb conjugations, and postpositions. For example, 'big boy' is बड़ा लड़का (masculine adjective form), while 'big girl' is बड़ी लड़की (feminine adjective form).

sections.title: Masculine Noun Patterns
sections.explanation: Most masculine nouns in Hindi fall into two categories. Type 1 masculine nouns end in -ा (aa) in their base form — these change their ending when they become plural or are followed by a postposition. For example, लड़का (boy) becomes लड़के (boys). Type 2 masculine nouns end in any other vowel or a consonant — these do not change form in the plural. For example, आदमी (man) stays आदमी in the plural, and घर (house) stays घर.
sections.tip: If a masculine noun ends in -ा, expect it to change to -े in the oblique case and plural. If it ends in anything else, its form stays the same.

examples.en: boy → boys — Type 1: -ा ending changes to -े in plural
room → rooms — Type 1: another -ा noun changing to -े
man → men — Type 2: no ending change in plural
house → houses — Type 2: consonant-ending noun, form stays the same

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of कमरा (room)?
exercises.explanation: कमरा is a Type 1 masculine noun ending in -ा. In the plural, the -ा changes to -े, giving कमरे (rooms). This is one of the most common patterns in Hindi.

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of आदमी (man)?
exercises.explanation: आदमी is a Type 2 masculine noun — it does not end in -ा, so its form does not change in the plural. Context and verbs indicate whether it means 'man' or 'men'.

exercises.prompt: Which ending is the hallmark of Type 1 masculine nouns?
exercises.explanation: Type 1 masculine nouns end in -ा in their base (direct singular) form. This -ा ending changes to -े in the oblique case and in the plural. Examples include लड़का, कमरा, and बेटा.

sections.title: Feminine Noun Patterns
sections.explanation: Feminine nouns also have two main types. Type 1 feminine nouns end in -ी (ii) — in the plural, this changes to -ियाँ. For example, लड़की (girl) becomes लड़कियाँ (girls). Type 2 feminine nouns end in any other vowel or a consonant — these add -एँ in the plural. For example, मेज़ (table) becomes मेज़ें (tables), and किताब (book) becomes किताबें (books). Some common exceptions exist: माँ (mother) becomes माँएँ, and बहन (sister) becomes बहनें.
sections.tip: Feminine plurals always add a nasal sound: -ियाँ for Type 1 and -एँ for Type 2. The chandrabindu (ँ) or anunasik marks this nasalization.

examples.en: bird → birds — -इया ending: just add nasalization (-ियाँ)
book → books — Type 2: consonant ending, add -एँ
table → tables — Type 2: consonant ending, add -एँ

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of लड़की (girl)?
exercises.explanation: लड़की is a Type 1 feminine noun ending in -ी. In the plural, -ी changes to -ियाँ, giving लड़कियाँ (girls). This pattern applies to most feminine nouns ending in -ी.

exercises.prompt: What is the plural of किताब (book)?
exercises.explanation: किताब is a Type 2 feminine noun ending in a consonant. The plural is formed by adding -एँ, resulting in किताबें (books). This pattern applies to feminine nouns that do not end in -ी.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a feminine noun?
exercises.explanation: मेज़ (table) is feminine in Hindi. लड़का (boy) and घर (house) are both masculine. The gender of inanimate objects like मेज़ must simply be memorized.
